section: Sources of Data and Methods of Estimation
text: $C_1$ and $C_2$ : The equation was derived from (a) preliminary measurements of $\mathrm{NaBF}_4$ ,<sup>4</sup> and (b) assuming that the temperature variation of viscosity for $\mathrm{NaBF}_4$ is equal to that of NaI.<sup>5</sup> Given the rather large uncertainty, the contribution of NaF (in $C_1$ ) to the viscosity may be considered negligible.
From the reported viscosity measurements<sup>1</sup> of the system $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - UF_4}$ , two trends can be observed:
(a) for LiF concentrations of 60 mole $\%$ or greater, substitution of $\mathrm{UF_4}$ for $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ (at const. temp.) causes an increase in viscosity,

(b) increasing LiF from 60 to 70 mole $\%$ , at const. temp. and at const. $\mathrm{UF_4}$ concentration, decreases the viscosity by, at most, a factor of $1/2$ ; for most compositions the factor is closer to $3/4$ .

The data and trends observed for the system $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - UF_4}$ can serve to predict reliably (i.e., to within $25\%$ ) the viscosities in the slightly different system, $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - MF_4}$ (M is Th and/or U).
